1: The word-event formula introduces two closely related but distinct oracles: against the 'prince' (nasi) of Tyre (Ezekiel 28:1-10) and against the 'king' (melek) of Tyre (Ezekiel 28:11-19). The distinction between nasi and melek may reflect different aspects of the ruler's identity (his political vs. cosmic/mythological role) or may address different figures (the ruling king and an idealized/mythological Tyrian ruler).
2: 'Say to the prince of Tyre: Because your heart is proud and you have said, I am a god; I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the sea — but you are a man, and not a god' — Ithobaal III (c.591-573 BCE), the reigning Tyrian king during Nebuchadnezzar's siege, and the Tyrian royal ideology of divine or semi-divine status. Phoenician kings claimed the patronage and protection of Baal, Melqart (the Tyrian deity), and El; the claim to 'sit in the seat of gods' reflects this ideology. The 'seat of gods' (moshav Elohim) in the heart of the sea: Tyre's island location was interpreted as a quasi-divine dwelling — the sea as the realm of the divine.
3: 'Behold, you are wiser than Daniel — no secret is hidden from you' — the reference to Daniel (Dan'el) is likely the same ancient figure as in Ezekiel 14:14: the legendary righteous and wise judge known from Ugaritic literature (the Aqhat epic, 14th-13th century BCE), where Dan'el (da-ni-il) is a righteous king renowned for judging the cause of the widow and orphan. The sarcastic comparison is rhetorically effective: the prince claims wisdom surpassing even the legendary Dan'el, but this very pride of wisdom is his indictment.
4: 'By your wisdom and understanding you have gained wealth for yourself and accumulated gold and silver in your treasuries' — the Tyrian prince's genuine commercial wisdom is acknowledged: he did achieve extraordinary wealth through real skill. This acknowledgment makes the indictment more pointed: the pride is not delusional but based on real achievement. The conversion of genuine skill into divine self-claim is the theological problem — what begins as competence becomes arrogance when it displaces recognition of the Creator.
5: 'By your great wisdom and in your trading you have increased your wealth, and your heart has become proud because of your wealth' — the spiritual mechanism of the fall: wisdom → trading → wealth → pride (gevah lev, proud heart). The Proverbs tradition consistently warns against the proud heart that results from prosperity (Proverbs 16:18; Ezekiel 18:12). The Tyrian prince's case is the paradigmatic example of this spiritual trajectory: legitimate commercial success generates illegitimate divine self-claim.
6: 'Therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Because you have set your heart as the heart of a god' — the precise charge: not merely that he claimed divine status rhetorically, but that he mentally and functionally placed himself in the category of deity. The 'heart as a god's heart' (lev levav Elohim) is the interior spiritual movement that precedes the outward claim. YHWH's judgment is addressed to the interior disposition, not merely the verbal claim.
7: 'Behold, I will bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of the nations' — the 'most ruthless of the nations' (araritzei goyim) is Ezekiel's recurring description of Babylon (Ezekiel 26:7; Ezekiel 30:11; Ezekiel 31:12; Ezekiel 32:12). The Babylonian army as the instrument of YHWH's judgment against Tyrian hubris: the city that claimed divine status will be subjected to the most violent of human military powers. 'They will draw their swords against your beauty and wisdom' — the wisdom and beauty that constituted the divine claim will be the specific target of the sword.
8: 'They will thrust you down into the Pit, and you will die the death of the slain in the heart of the seas' — the throne in the 'seat of the gods in the heart of the sea' (Ezekiel 28:2) becomes the grave in the 'heart of the seas.' The spatial inversion is complete: the divine dwelling becomes the place of death. 'The Pit' (shachat, H7845, pit/grave/corruption) is the underworld; dying in the sea rather than in the Pit is a different kind of death — without burial, without honor, lost in the element that was the prince's source of pride.
9: 'Will you still say, I am a god, before the one who kills you? But you will be a mere man in the hand of the one who wounds you' — the moment of the claim's falsification: death itself proves that the divine claim was false. The contrast between claiming to be a god (Elohim) and being 'a mere man' (adam) in the hand of one who wounds you is the rhetorical climax of the first oracle. Death is the universal human experience that destroys all pretensions to divinity.
10: 'You will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of foreigners, for I have spoken' — the shame of dying 'the death of the uncircumcised' (mot arelim): in Phoenician culture, circumcision was practiced (Herodotus 2.104 notes that Phoenicians practiced circumcision along with Egyptians and Ethiopians). To die 'the death of the uncircumcised' may mean dying without the honor of proper ritual and burial, or dying as those who are outside the covenant community — the ultimate social and cultic humiliation for one who claimed divine status.
11: The second oracle: against the 'king' (melek) of Tyre, using cosmic-mythological imagery unprecedented in the OT prophetic literature. The shift from nasi to melek may signal a shift in focus: from the present historical ruler to the idealized/primordial figure behind Tyrian royal ideology. The oracle draws on motifs from the broader ancient Near Eastern mythological tradition while subverting their claims.
12: 'You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty' — tochnit (H8508, pattern/plan/seal) of perfection: the figure who is the perfect exemplar of what beauty and wisdom should be. The phrase 'full of wisdom and perfect in beauty' combines the two qualities the prince-oracle addressed — they have cosmic proportions in the king-oracle. The king of Tyre as the cosmic embodiment of the perfection that the creation intended.
13: 'You were in Eden, the garden of God; every precious stone adorned you' — the explicit Eden location: the king of Tyre is placed in Gan Eden (hagan-Eden, the garden of God/Eden). The list of nine precious stones (ruby, topaz, diamond/crystal, beryl, onyx, jasper, sapphire, turquoise, emerald) plus gold and the crafted settings corresponds to (and partially overlaps with) the high priest's breastplate stones (Exodus 28:17-20). 'On the day you were created' (beyom hibbar'echa) — this is a created being, not eternal: the king of Tyre/the figure behind him was created, placed in Eden, adorned with precious stones.
14: 'You were the anointed guardian cherub (cherub mimshach hasokek); I had appointed you. You were on the holy mountain of God; you walked among the fiery stones' — the most theologically dense verse in the oracle. Three identifications: (1) anointed guardian cherub — a cherub given covenant anointing, placed as the guardian/covering cherub (cf. the cherubim who guard Eden in Genesis 3:24); (2) on the holy mountain of God — the cosmic mountain of divine assembly (cf. Psalms 48:2; Isaiah 14:13); (3) walking among fiery stones (beevenei-esh hithhalakta) — the divine throne-room phenomena of Ezekiel 1 (fiery stones in the vicinity of the chariot-throne). Whether this describes the historical Tyrian king in cosmic-mythological terms or an angelic being whose corruption underlies Tyre's spiritual reality has generated extensive theological discussion.
15: 'You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until iniquity was found in you' — the perfect creation narrative: created without moral flaw, the iniquity found (passive: 'was found') as something that emerged from within. The phrase parallels both the pre-fall Adam (created good) and the Serpent's corrupting influence in the garden. The transition from blameless to iniquitous is the core of the oracle and mirrors the transition in Adam/Eve narratives.
16: 'Through the abundance of your trade you were filled inwardly with violence, and you sinned; so I cast you out as profane from the mountain of God' — the specific mechanism of the fall: commerce generating violence. rechullatechu (your trade/trafficking, H7404) as the source of the interior violence (chamas, H2555). The commercial activity that was Tyre's pride became the spiritual corrupting force. 'I cast you out as profane' (chalal, H2490, defiled/profaned) from the mountain of God — the expulsion from the divine mountain uses the language of ritual defilement: the sacred space cannot tolerate the profaned presence.
17: 'Your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor. I have cast you to the ground; I have exposed you before kings to feast their eyes on you' — the pride-fall pattern: beauty → pride → corrupted wisdom → public humiliation before kings. The 'feast their eyes on you' (ra'o becha) is the public spectacle of the humiliated proud: the kings of the earth watch the exposure of the one who claimed to be above them.
18: 'By the abundance of your iniquities and the wrongdoing of your trade you defiled your sanctuaries; therefore I have brought fire from the midst of you' — the fire from within: not external destruction but internal combustion. The 'sanctuaries' (miqddashecha) that were defiled — the sacred spaces of Tyre, including the famous temple of Melqart described by Herodotus (2.44). 'It has consumed you' — Tyre consumed by its own internal fire, the violence that trade generated eventually destroying the trader.
19: 'All who knew you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a horror and shall be no more forever' — the same closing formula as the Ezekiel 27 dirge (Ezekiel 27:36): appalled (shamemu) at the one who was the seal of perfection; horror (ballahot); no more forever. The king-oracle closes with the same language of permanent dissolution as the ship-allegory, tying the two chapters together in a single extended meditation on Tyrian greatness and its judgment.
20: A brief oracle transition — the word-event formula marks the shift to the Sidon oracle. Sidon was the senior Phoenician city (Tyre was traditionally its daughter-colony) and was politically and commercially linked to Tyre throughout the period. The oracle against Sidon is much shorter than the Tyre oracles, reflecting Sidon's lesser commercial and cultic significance in Ezekiel's period.
21: 'Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against it' — Sidon (H6721, possibly connected to 'fishing'), the city 40 km north of Tyre on the Phoenician coast. The sim panim el (set-face) formula is the standard opening for the OAN oracles (cf. Ezekiel 6:2; Ezekiel 13:17; Ezekiel 25:2; Ezekiel 28:2; Ezekiel 29:2). Sidon was involved in the anti-Babylonian coalition referenced in Jeremiah 27:3 (Sidon sent envoys to Jerusalem along with Edom, Moab, Ammon, and Tyre to coordinate resistance to Babylon).
22: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: Behold, I am against you, Sidon; I will be glorified in your midst. They will know that I am the LORD when I execute judgments on her and show myself holy in her' — the Sidon oracle is primarily a recognition-formula oracle. The key terms: nikhbadti ('I will be glorified/honored') and weniqdashti ('I will show myself holy') — YHWH's self-disclosure through Sidon's judgment. The oracle against Sidon is less detailed than those against Tyre because its primary purpose is to complete the Phoenician city-state section of the OAN before the Egyptian section begins.
23: 'I will send pestilence into her and blood into her streets; the slain will fall in her midst by the sword that is against her on every side; then they will know that I am the LORD' — pestilence (dever) and sword as the standard dual instruments of urban judgment. The recognition formula closes the brief oracle. Sidon's historical defeat at the hands of Artaxerxes III in 345 BCE (the city was burned and 40,000 inhabitants reportedly died) is sometimes cited as a fulfillment, though the timeframe differs significantly from Ezekiel's context.
24: 'There will no longer be a painful brier or a piercing thorn from any of the surrounding peoples who showed contempt for Israel' — the transition from judgment-oracle to restoration-promise for Israel. The 'brier and thorn' (sillon mam'ir and qotz mak'ov) language echoes Numbers 33:55 (the Canaanites who remain will be 'pricks in your eyes and thorns in your sides'). The judgment of Tyre, Sidon, and the other neighboring peoples eliminates the persistent hostile pressure on Israel from its commercial and political neighbors.
25: 'Thus says the Lord GOD: When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they have been scattered, and show myself holy in them in the sight of the nations, then they will dwell in their own land that I gave to my servant Jacob' — the restoration promise that closes the entire OAN section (Ezekiel 25-28): the gathering of scattered Israel, YHWH's sanctification displayed before the nations, and the return to the Jacobite inheritance. The 'servant Jacob' (avdi ya'aqov) is the patriarchal ground of the land promise — the same promise that undergirds the restoration promises of Ezekiel 34-37.
26: 'They will dwell in it safely and build houses and plant vineyards. They will live with confidence when I have executed judgments on all their neighbors who have shown contempt for them; then they will know that I am the LORD their God' — the picture of the restored Israel: security (betach, H983, in safety), building houses, planting vineyards — the standard covenant-blessing formula for settled abundance (Isaiah 65:21-22; Amos 9:14). The causal link: the security of restored Israel is contingent on the judgment of the hostile neighbors. The recognition formula here is 'I am the LORD their God' — with the possessive: after all the OAN oracles in which YHWH revealed himself to foreign nations through their judgments, he reveals himself to Israel as their specific covenant Lord.
